network_scanning.content: Network scanning is the systematic probing of networked systems to discover hosts, open ports, running services, and operating system information. Nmap is the industry standard, offering multiple scan types: TCP SYN scan (-sS, default, stealthy), TCP connect scan (-sT, complete handshake), UDP scan (-sU, slow due to stateless protocol), FIN/NULL/Xmas scans (-sF, -sN, -sX, for firewall evasion), and version detection (-sV). Masscan is the fastest port scanner, capable of scanning the entire IPv4 internet in under 6 minutes on appropriate hardware. Zmap is another high-speed scanner designed for single-port internet-wide scans. Best practices: always have written authorization before scanning, use rate limiting to avoid DoS, verify scan results manually for critical findings, and handle scan data securely as it may contain sensitive infrastructure information.

web_scanning.content: Web application scanning involves discovering endpoints, parameters, and vulnerabilities in web applications. Directory brute-forcing uses wordlists (SecLists, dirbuster, raft) with tools like ffuf (fast), gobuster, or dirsearch to discover hidden resources. Parameter discovery with tools like ParamSpider and Arjun identifies undocumented parameters. SQL injection detection should test both error-based (single quote, double quote, URL encoding) and blind (time-based, boolean-based) variants. Automate with sqlmap for confirmation and exploitation but verify findings manually. XSS testing covers reflected, stored, DOM-based, and mXSS variants. API testing requires understanding of authentication mechanisms, rate limits, and input validation patterns. Always test from an authenticated perspective for applications with access controls.

vulnerability_assessment.content: Vulnerability assessment systematically identifies, quantifies, and prioritizes vulnerabilities in systems. CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) provides standardized identifiers for publicly known vulnerabilities. CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) scores vulnerabilities on severity (0-10) based on attack vector, complexity, privileges required, user interaction, scope, and impacts on confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Risk analysis combines vulnerability severity with asset criticality and threat likelihood to prioritize remediation. Automated scanners (Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS) provide coverage but have false positives - manual validation is essential. A mature VA program includes regular scheduled scans, authentication-based scanning for deeper coverage, and a remediation tracking process with SLAs based on severity levels.

penetration_testing.content: Penetration testing follows a structured methodology simulating real-world attacks. The PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard) defines seven phases: Pre-engagement Interactions, Intelligence Gathering, Threat Modeling, Vulnerability Analysis, Exploitation, Post-Exploitation, and Reporting. The OWASP Testing Guide provides web-specific methodology with 90+ test cases. Reconnaissance divides into passive (OSINT, DNS enumeration, Shodan, Google dorking, social media) and active (port scanning, service enumeration, banner grabbing). Exploitation should be targeted and documented, using both automated tools (Metasploit, Core Impact) and manual techniques. Persistence mechanisms include backdoored binaries, scheduled tasks, cron jobs, SSH keys, and web shells. Post-exploitation collects credentials, sensitive data, and lateral movement opportunities. Exfiltration testing validates data loss prevention controls.

reverse_engineering.content: Reverse engineering analyzes software to understand its behavior, recover source logic, or find vulnerabilities. Static analysis examines code without execution: IDA Pro (industry-standard disassembler with decompiler), Ghidra (free, open-source NSA tool with Sleigh decompiler), Binary Ninja (modern IL-based analysis), and radare2/rizin (command-line framework). Dynamic analysis observes execution: x64dbg (Windows user-mode), WinDbg (Windows kernel-mode), GDB/LDB (Linux), and LLDB (macOS). Assembly knowledge required: x86/x64 (Intel/AT&T syntax), ARM (AArch32/AArch64/Thumb), and increasingly RISC-V and WebAssembly. Common techniques: import/export table analysis, string extraction, cross-reference tracking, debugger detection bypass (anti-reverse), packer/unpacker identification (PEiD, Detect It Easy), and API monitoring (API Monitor, strace, ltrace, Process Monitor).

malware_analysis.content: Malware analysis determines the functionality, origin, and impact of malicious software. Static analysis examines files without execution: hash lookup (VirusTotal), packer detection, string analysis, PE/ELF/Mach-O structure analysis, and signature matching. Dynamic analysis observes behavior in sandboxed environments (Cuckoo Sandbox, CAPE, ANY.RUN). Behavioral analysis documents file system changes, registry modifications, network connections, process injection, and persistence mechanisms. YARA rules create signatures based on string patterns, byte sequences, and metadata for malware classification and detection. Memory analysis extracts artifacts from RAM dumps using Volatility 3. Key malware techniques to recognize: API hooking, DLL injection, process hollowing, reflective DLL loading, rootkit hiding techniques (DKOM, inline hooks), and C2 communication patterns (HTTP beaconing, DNS tunneling, domain generation algorithms).

forensics.content: Digital forensics investigates cyber incidents through systematic evidence collection and analysis. Disk forensics examines storage media: acquisition (forensic images in E01, AFF, or raw format with write blockers), file system analysis (NTFS, ext4, APFS - MFT, inodes, journal), file carving (recovering deleted files by identifying signatures with tools like foremost, scalpel, photorec), and timeline analysis (super-timelines with log2timeline/plaso). Memory forensics analyzes RAM dumps with Volatility 3 (profiles for OS version, plugins for processes, network connections, registry hives, loaded DLLs, injected code). Network forensics examines pcap captures: protocol analysis (Wireshark, tshark), flow analysis (Zeek), and artifacts extraction (files, certificates, credentials). Mobile forensics extracts evidence from iOS and Android devices via logical, file system, and physical acquisition methods.

osint.content: Open Source Intelligence gathers information from publicly available sources. Shodan searches internet-connected devices (webcams, routers, ICS/SCADA systems, databases) with filters for port, service, location, and organization. Google dorking uses advanced search operators (site:, filetype:, intitle:, inurl:, cache:) to find exposed sensitive information. Social media intelligence (SOCMINT) gathers information from LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook, Instagram for corporate recon, person of interest investigation, and social engineering preparation. WHOIS lookups reveal domain registration details (registrar, nameservers, dates, contacts - increasingly redacted due to GDPR). DNS enumeration (dnsrecon, dig, nslookup, Amass) discovers subdomains, mail servers, and zone transfers. Certificate transparency logs (crt.sh) reveal subdomains from SSL certificate issuance records.

exploit_development.content: Exploit development transforms vulnerabilities into functional exploits. Buffer overflows remain relevant in IoT and embedded systems: overwrite the return address on the stack to redirect execution. Stack-based exploitation: control EIP/RIP with a pattern (pattern_create/offset), inject shellcode (avoid bad characters, encode with msfvenom), and set return address to shellcode location (JMP ESP or ROP). ROP (Return-Oriented Programming) chains gadgets (sequences ending with ret) to execute arbitrary logic without injecting code, bypassing NX/DEP. Return-to-libc calls system functions (system, execve) from libc libraries. Heap spraying fills the heap with NOP sleds and shellcode then triggers a use-after-free. SEH overwrite exploits Structured Exception Handler chains on Windows. Modern mitigations include ASLR (address space layout randomization), DEP (data execution prevention), SEHOP, CFG (control flow guard), CET (control-flow enforcement technology), and stack canaries.

cryptography.content: Cryptography provides confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation. Symmetric encryption (same key for encrypt/decrypt): AES (Rijndael, modes ECB, CBC, GCM - GCM provides authenticated encryption), ChaCha20 (stream cipher, fast in software, used in TLS). Asymmetric encryption (public/private key pairs): RSA (factoring-based, key sizes 2048+ bits), ECC (elliptic curve, smaller keys, ECDH for key exchange, ECDSA for signatures). Hashing (one-way, deterministic): SHA-2 family (SHA-256, SHA-512), SHA-3 (Keccak), BLAKE2/3. Digital signatures prove authenticity and integrity using asymmetric keys. TLS (Transport Layer Security) provides secure channels with handshake (key exchange and authentication), record (encrypted data), and alert protocols. PKI manages certificates (X.509), CAs, certificate chains, and revocation (CRL, OCSP). Common attacks: padding oracle, timing attacks, meet-in-the-middle, birthday attacks, side-channel attacks.

social_engineering.content: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Phishing is the most common vector: crafting convincing emails that mimic legitimate communications, using urgency or authority to bypass rational evaluation. Spear phishing targets specific individuals with personalized content. Vishing (voice phishing) uses phone calls with caller ID spoofing. SMiShing uses SMS messages. Pretexting creates a fabricated scenario (IT support, vendor, auditor) to extract information. Baiting offers something enticing (free USB drives, downloads) that contains malware. Physical security assessment tests tailgating (following authorized personnel through secured doors), dumpster diving, and badge cloning. Defense includes security awareness training, multi-factor authentication, reporting procedures, and simulated phishing exercises to measure susceptibility.

wireless_security.content: Wireless security assesses WiFi, Bluetooth, RFID, and other wireless protocols. WPA2 uses CCMP/AES encryption with a 4-way handshake. Attacks: capturing the handshake (aireplay-ng deauth attack) then offline cracking with aircrack-ng, hashcat, or John the Ripper. WPA3 uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) handshake resistant to offline dictionary attacks, but the Dragonblood attack revealed implementation vulnerabilities. WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup) PIN brute-force (Pixie Dust attack) can recover the PSK in hours. Bluetooth LE attacks include BTLE sniffing (Ubertooth, nRF Sniffer), MAC address tracking, and connection hijacking. RFID cloning captures and reproduces low-frequency (125kHz) and high-frequency (13.56MHz, MIFARE) tags. Defense: WPA3-Enterprise, disable WPS, use 802.1X with EAP-TLS, wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS), and RF monitoring.

cloud_security.content: Cloud security assessment evaluates the security posture of cloud infrastructure. IAM analysis identifies over-permissive roles, unused credentials, and cross-account trust. Common AWS misconfigurations: S3 buckets with public read/write, security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress, IAM users with AdministratorAccess, unencrypted EBS/RDS volumes, Lambda functions with excessive roles. Azure security involves Azure AD configuration, RBAC role assignments, NSG rules, Key Vault access policies, and Managed Identity security. GCP assessment covers IAM roles (primitive vs predefined), VPC firewall rules, Cloud Storage bucket ACLs, and service account key management. Automated tools: ScoutSuite (multi-cloud), Prowler (AWS), CloudSploit, pacu (AWS exploitation framework), and Cloudsplaining (IAM analysis). The Cloud Security Alliance provides the Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) for comprehensive assessment.

mobile_security.content: Mobile security assessment evaluates iOS and Android applications for vulnerabilities. Android app reversing: decompile APK with jadx or APKTool to examine AndroidManifest.xml, resources, and smali code. Common issues: insecure data storage (SharedPreferences, SQLite without encryption), exposed content providers, improper WebView configuration (JavaScript enabled, file:// access), insecure network communication (cleartext HTTP), and hardcoded credentials. iOS app analysis: decrypt IPA (if App Store binary), disassemble with Hopper or Ghidra, examine Info.plist, check for insecure NSUserDefaults, improper TLS validation, and insecure data protection. Frida is the most powerful dynamic instrumentation tool - hook JavaScript into running apps to bypass certificate pinning, intercept function calls, and modify runtime behavior. Objection is a Frida wrapper for mobile security testing.

web_application_firewall.content: WAF bypass techniques circumvent web application firewall rules. SQL injection bypass: use comment obfuscation (/**/), case variation, URL encoding, double encoding, null bytes, hexadecimal representation, and alternative operators. XSS filter evasion: event handler alternatives (onmouseover, onfocus, onerror), encoding variations, polyglots, and DOM clobbering. Rule evasion: parameter pollution (adding multiple same-named parameters), HTTP method switching, protocol smuggling, chunked transfer encoding, and unicode normalization attacks. Rate limiting bypass: IP rotation (proxies, VPN, Tor), distributed attacks, slow-rate attacks, and timing-based bypass of non-threshold limits. Defense: ModSecurity (open-source WAF with OWASP CRS), Cloudflare WAF, AWS WAF, Azure WAF, F5 BIG-IP ASM, and Imperva. Combine WAF with runtime application self-protection (RASP) for defense in depth.

active_directory.content: Active Directory (AD) is Microsoft's directory service for Windows domain networks, a frequent target in enterprise compromises. Security assessment covers LDAP enumeration, Kerberos attacks, privilege escalation paths, and lateral movement. Kerberos attack types: Kerberoasting (request TGS tickets for service accounts and crack offline), AS-REP roasting (find accounts without pre-authentication), Golden Ticket (forge TGT with KRBTGT hash), Silver Ticket (forge TGS for specific services), and DCSync (replicate directory via DRSUAPI to extract password hashes). Privilege escalation identifies attack paths: ACL abuse (GenericAll, GenericWrite, WriteDACL), admin SDHolder propagation, Group Policy preference passwords, and unconstrained delegation abuse. Tools: BloodHound (graph-based attack path analysis), PowerView, Impacket, Rubeus, Mimikatz, and CrackMapExec.

threat_intelligence.content: Threat intelligence provides actionable information about current and emerging threats. The threat hunting process: form hypotheses based on intelligence, search for IoCs across endpoints and network logs, investigate anomalies, and document findings. IoC types: file hashes (MD5, SHA1, SHA256), IP addresses (C2 servers, scanners), domain names (DGA, typosquatting), URLs/paths, registry keys, mutex names, and YARA rules. Threat modeling methodologies: STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege), DREAD (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected Users, Discoverability), PASTA, and OCTAVE. The MITRE ATT&CK framework categorizes adversary tactics and techniques across 14 enterprise tactics from Reconnaissance to Impact. The Diamond Model analyzes intrusions through adversary, infrastructure, capability, and victim relationships.

incident_response.content: Incident response (IR) manages the aftermath of security breaches. The NIST SP 800-61 framework defines four phases: Preparation (build IR plan, assemble team, acquire tools), Detection and Analysis (identify incident, determine scope, preserve evidence), Containment, Eradication, and Recovery (stop the attack, remove threat, restore systems), and Post-Incident Activity (lessons learned, update procedures). Containment strategies: disconnect affected systems (short-term), implement firewall blocks, disable compromised accounts, take forensic images. Eradication removes malicious artifacts, patches exploited vulnerabilities, resets credentials. Recovery restores from clean backups, monitors for re-infection, verifies system integrity. Reporting is critical: internal (management, legal, PR), regulatory (if data breach laws apply), and law enforcement. Post-mortems should be blameless, focusing on process improvements.

secure_coding.content: Secure coding prevents vulnerabilities at the source code level. The OWASP Top 10 lists the most critical web application security risks: Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, Injection, Insecure Design, Security Misconfiguration, Vulnerable Components, Auth Failures, Data Integrity Failures, Logging/Monitoring Failures, and SSRF. Input validation: validate on server-side (never trust client-side only), use allowlists over blocklists, validate type, length, format, and range. Output encoding prevents XSS: context-aware encoding for HTML (HTML entity), JavaScript (string literal), CSS, and URL contexts. Authentication: store passwords with slow salted hashes (bcrypt, Argon2id), enforce password policies, implement MFA, use secure session management (HTTPS-only cookies, SameSite, httpOnly). Access control: implement server-side checks, deny by default, principle of least privilege, and verify authorization for every request.

api_security.content: API security protects REST, GraphQL, and gRPC APIs from attacks. Key concerns: authentication and authorization (verify identity and permissions on every request, validate JWT signatures and expiration, implement proper scopes), injection attacks (SQL, NoSQL, command injection via API parameters - use parameterized queries and input validation), batch attacks and mass assignment (rate limit and filter request payloads against allowed fields), broken object level authorization (BOLA - verify user owns the requested resource), and sensitive data exposure (encrypt in transit with TLS, encrypt sensitive fields at rest, avoid leaking details in error messages). GraphQL security concerns: deeply nested queries (implement query depth limiting and cost analysis), introspection leakage (disable in production), and batch resolution abuse (rate limit resolvers). API gateways (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Apigee) centralize authentication, rate limiting, logging, and request transformation.

container_security.content: Container security spans the entire lifecycle from image creation to runtime. Image security: scan images for known vulnerabilities (Trivy, Grype, Docker Scout, Snyk), use minimal base images (distroless, Alpine, scratch), scan with both OS package and language-specific vulnerability databases, sign images with Sigstore/Cosign to guarantee provenance and integrity. Build-time security: run containers as non-root (USER directive), avoid privileged containers, set read-only root filesystem, drop unnecessary Linux capabilities (--cap-drop=ALL, then add required), use secrets mounts instead of environment variables for credentials. Runtime security: container runtime security (Falco, Tracee) monitors system calls for anomalous behavior, SELinux/AppArmor/seccomp profiles restrict syscall access, Pod Security Standards in Kubernetes enforce baseline/restricted policies, network policies isolate pod traffic, and OPA/Gatekeeper enforce admission control policies.

zero_trust.content: Zero Trust architecture assumes breach and verifies every request regardless of source. Core principles: never trust, always verify - treat every access request as if it originates from an open network; assume breach - design for the worst case, limit blast radius; and least privilege - grant only the minimum access required. Implementation pillars: identity (strong authentication with continuous verification, risk-based conditional access), devices (device health checks, compliance policy enforcement before access), networks (micro-segmentation, encrypt all traffic, network visibility and analytics), applications (application discovery, access decisions based on multiple signals, adaptive access, app-layer security), data (data classification, encryption at rest and in transit, DLP, DRM). The NIST SP 800-207 standard defines zero trust architecture components including Policy Decision Point (PDP), Policy Enforcement Point (PEP), and Policy Administrator (PA).

devsecops.content: DevSecOps integrates security into the DevOps pipeline. Principles: shift left (test security early in development), automate security checks, and make security everyone's responsibility. CI/CD security integration: SAST (Static Application Security Testing - SonarQube, Semgrep, CodeQL) runs on every code commit to find vulnerabilities without executing code; DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing - OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite) scans running applications; SCA (Software Composition Analysis - OWASP Dependency-Check, Snyk, Dependabot) detects vulnerable dependencies. Secret management: never commit secrets to repositories, use vaults (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), and implement secret scanning in CI (GitHub secret scanning, GitLeaks, TruffleHog). Supply chain security: sign artifacts with Sigstore, verify dependency integrity (SLSA framework, in-toto attestations), maintain SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) with CycloneDX or SPDX.

password_cracking.content: Password cracking recovers plaintext passwords from hashed credential dumps. Hashcat is the modern standard GPU-accelerated cracker supporting 320+ hash types (MD5, SHA1-512, NTLM, bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2, LM, Kerberos, and many more). Attack modes: dictionary (-a 0), combinator (-a 1), mask (-a 3, brute force with patterns), hybrid wordlist+mask (-a 6), and association (-a 9). GPU acceleration: AMD (OpenCL) and NVIDIA (CUDA) provide massive parallelism - modern GPUs achieve billions of MD5 hashes per second. Wordlists: rockyou.txt (14M passwords from 2009 breach, still effective due to human password reuse), Have I Been Pwned (600M+ unique passwords), CrackStation (1.5B+). Rule-based attacks (best64, dive.rule, OneRuleToRuleThemAll) transform base words through suffixing, prefixing, leet speak, capitalization, and substitution. Mask attacks target known patterns: ?u?l?l?l?l?d?d?d?d for capital+lowercase+digits.

database_security.content: Database security protects data at rest and in motion. SQL injection remains a top risk: test for in-band (error-based, UNION), blind (boolean-based, time-based), and out-of-band injection. NoSQL injection affects MongoDB ($where, $gt, $ne operators), CouchDB, and Cassandra - test JSON injection in API parameters. Database hardening: remove default accounts, change default ports, apply least privilege (GRANT only necessary permissions), encrypt at rest (TDE for MSSQL, encryptfs for MySQL, pgcrypto for PostgreSQL), encrypt in transit (TLS connections with mutual auth), enable audit logging, set connection rate limits, and use network isolation (dedicated VLAN, firewall rules for port 1433/3306/5432). Advanced protection: database activity monitoring (DAM), dynamic data masking, and database firewall solutions.

iot_security.content: IoT security assessments target embedded devices and their ecosystems. Firmware analysis: extract firmware (binwalk, firmware-mod-kit), identify filesystem (squashfs, jffs2, ubifs, cramfs), analyze for hardcoded credentials, backdoors, and vulnerable services (telnet, insecure web servers), and emulate with QEMU for dynamic analysis. Hardware attacks access debug interfaces: JTAG (boundary scan, CPU debug via OpenOCD), SWD (Serial Wire Debug on ARM Cortex), UART (serial console, often at 115200 baud with no authentication). Flash reading: SPI flash chip desoldering/clip-on reading with flashrom or Bus Pirate, eMMC reading via SD card adapter, and NAND flash read with chip-off techniques. Side-channel attacks: power analysis (SPA/DPA, ChipWhisperer), electromagnetic analysis, timing attacks. Protocol analysis: SPI, I2C, CAN bus, Modbus, MQTT, Zigbee, Z-Wave.

blockchain_security.content: Blockchain security focuses on smart contract vulnerabilities, consensus mechanisms, and DeFi protocols. Smart contract audits review Solidity code for common vulnerabilities: reentrancy (re-entering a function before previous invocation completes - the DAO hack), unchecked external calls (low-level call/send/delegatecall), integer overflow/underflow (SafeMath in pre-0.8 Solidity), access control issues (tx.origin vs msg.sender), flash loan attacks (uncollateralized borrowing manipulating oracle prices), and sandwich attacks (front-running trades on DEXes). Consensus attack vectors: 51% attack, nothing-at-stake, long-range attacks on PoS. DeFi security: price oracle manipulation (flash loan attacks on Uniswap/Chainlink), liquidity pool impermanent loss, vault/rebase mechanism exploits, and cross-chain bridge vulnerabilities. Key tools: Slither (static analysis), Echidna (fuzzing), Mythril (symbolic execution), Foundry (Rust-based testing with fuzzing), and Certora (formal verification).

ai_security.content: AI security addresses vulnerabilities in machine learning systems. Adversarial examples: subtle perturbations to inputs that cause ML models to misclassify - evasion attacks on image classifiers (Fast Gradient Sign Method, Projected Gradient Descent, Carlini-Wagner), text classifiers (synonym substitution, character manipulation), and malware detectors. Model stealing: extract model parameters or architecture through query access, using equation-solving attacks on decision trees, knock-off attacks on neural networks, or membership inference (determine if a data point was in training set). Data poisoning: injecting malicious samples into training data to create backdoors or degrade model performance - label flipping, gradient manipulation, trigger-based backdoors. Prompt injection in LLMs: direct (override system instructions), indirect (inject via retrieved documents or tool outputs), and jailbreaking (role-play, hypotheticals, encoding tricks). Defense: adversarial training, input sanitization, differential privacy, certified defenses, prompt guards, and output filtering.

supply_chain.content: Supply chain security protects the software development and delivery pipeline from compromise. Dependency confusion: attackers publish packages with the same name as internal packages to public registries - build tools may prefer public over private when configured incorrectly. Typosquatting: publish packages with names similar to popular packages (request -> reqwest, crossenv -> cross-env). Compromised packages: attackers gain access to maintainer accounts or CI/CD pipelines (SolarWinds, CodeCov, event-stream incidents). SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) provides an inventory of all components - generate with CycloneDX or SPDX format, store alongside releases. SLSA (Supply chain Levels for Software Artifacts) framework defines build integrity levels from SLSA 1 (documented) to SLSA 4 (two-person review + hermetic, reproducible builds). Mitigations: pin dependency versions, use lockfiles, private registries for internal packages, verify package signatures, scan for known vulnerabilities, and sign releases with Sigstore.

red_team.content: Red team operations simulate advanced persistent threats to test an organization's detection and response capabilities. Mission planning: define rules of engagement, scope, and success criteria with blue team stakeholders. Reconnaissance and initial access: OSINT gathering, phishing campaigns, exploited public-facing applications, valid account compromise, or physical penetration. C2 (Command and Control) frameworks: Cobalt Strike (Malleable C2), Mythic (extensible agents, web UI), Covenant (open-source C#, .NET), Sliver (Golang, multi-protocol), and Havoc (modern, cross-platform). C2 evasion: domain fronting, CDN abuse, HTTPS beaconing with jitter, C2 over legitimate services (DNS tunneling, Microsoft 365, Google Drive, Notion). Evasion techniques: AMSI bypass, ETW patching, process injection (classic, APC, thread hijacking), indirect syscalls for EDR bypass, and sleep obfuscation. Lateral movement: pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, remote service creation, WinRM, PSRemoting, and WMI.

blue_team.content: Blue team operations defend against attacks through detection, prevention, and response. SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) rules correlate logs from endpoints, network devices, and applications - Splunk, Elastic Security, Microsoft Sentinel, QRadar, and Wazuh (open-source). EDR (Endpoint Detection and Response) monitors endpoints for malicious behavior: Windows Defender for Endpoint, CrowdStrike Falcon, SentinelOne, Carbon Black, and Velociraptor (open-source DFIR). Detection engineering creates and tunes detection rules using Sigma (generic signature format), YARA (file patterns), and SNORT/Suricata (network signatures). Threat hunting proactively searches for indicators of compromise using hypothesis-driven analysis, VT queries, and UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics). Deception technology deploys honeypots, honeytokens, and canary accounts to detect attackers.

post_exploitation.content: Post-exploitation actions after gaining initial access to a system. Linux privilege escalation: kernel exploits (CVE-2021-3493, CVE-2023-2640), misconfigured sudo entries, SUID binaries, capabilities (CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE), cron jobs, Path variable hijacking, NFS no_root_squash, and Docker group membership (mount host filesystem). Windows privilege escalation: unquoted service paths, weak service permissions, always install elevated, credential dumping (Mimikatz, SAM registry, LSASS, DPAPI), UAC bypass, scheduled task abuse, and kernel exploits. Persistence mechanisms: Linux - SSH authorized_keys, cron, systemd services, LD_PRELOAD, kernel modules; Windows - registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, services, WMI event subscriptions, COM hijacking, DLL search order hijacking, and bootkits. Data exfiltration: compress (7zip, tar), encrypt, split, exfiltrate via DNS (dnscat2, iodine), HTTPS (Google Drive API, S3), ICMP (icmpdoor), or physical media.

binary_exploitation.content: Binary exploitation attacks compiled programs, often requiring bypass of modern memory protection mechanisms. ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) randomizes memory addresses - bypass with information leaks (format string, heap address disclosure) or partial overwrite techniques. DEP/NX (Data Execution Prevention) marks memory pages non-executable - bypass via ROP/JOP/COP (Return/Jump/Call-Oriented Programming). Stack canaries detect buffer overflows - bypass via info leak (read canary value before overflow), or overwrite canary with same value during fork-based servers (canary is per-process). CFG (Control Flow Guard) validates indirect call targets - bypass via overwriting CFG bitmap, or using call targets already in the bitmap. CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) is Intel's hardware shadow stack. Kernel exploitation targets operating system vulnerabilities for privilege escalation. Heap exploitation: use-after-free, heap overflow, and heap spraying.

cryptographic_protocols.content: Modern cryptographic protocols provide secure communication with various security properties. TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) simplifies the handshake to one round trip (0-RTT for resumed sessions), removes insecure options (static RSA, RC4, 3DES, CBC mode ciphers), and uses AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) with X25519 key exchange. The Signal Protocol provides end-to-end encryption for messaging with forward secrecy (ephemeral keys per session) and post-compromise security (self-healing through ratcheting) - uses the X3DH key agreement and Double Ratchet algorithm. Noise Protocol Framework (used in WhatsApp, WireGuard) enables building custom secure protocols with pre-shared keys, handshake patterns, and hybrid forward secrecy. OPAQUE is a secure Password Authenticated Key Exchange (PAKE) that prevents offline dictionary attacks even if the server is compromised. PAKE protocols (SPAKE2, CPace, OPAQUE) enable password-based authentication without exposing password equivalents.
